where it may get used later for something, but it's going to be so old by February, there's no point in doing anything with it. So there's sort of loosey-goosey sort decisions made as I handle each thing that comes in. A question for you. So I notice a lot of people, as a matter of fact I know a lot of people in this room, but I notice a lot of people sort of in the GKSC world, they try to emulate the newsletter in their own business. It's my question to you, and a lot of them, many of them don't do a great job of absolutely emulating it. They sort of get it written structural, and they get it out, but it misses a lot of key element pieces. So what would you say are some of the top ones, if you had to say, here's like a little short list of things that make like a quick fix to that. Not that it's ever going to get to this level. I did it. I should have pointed it out. Glad you asked. It's the bold-faced item under each alpha. So what I would view as most important are the bold-faced items, which in this case is A, structurally is A, F, T, U. But when you say they don't get it, I think some of it's not really understanding what the hell it really is, but I think more of that is, you know, the two things I hear a lot is that they don't really think it's important. You know, they don't place as much importance on it as I do. And maybe I overvalue it. I don't know. But see, I think like the newsletters and the weekly faxes are the most important things, not the most important things we do, but certainly the most important thing I do. And they don't give enough thought to what are we trying to accomplish. What do we want? You know, it's more like, okay, there's five things they get for their money this month. They get a newsletter. They get a CD. So we got to get this shit out. We got to deliver this. Not what are the three or four things we want to accomplish this month? Not just the overriding list, but what are the three or four things we want to accomplish this month? What do we want to set up to happen next month? Who we should interview based on what we're trying to have happen this calendar quarter? You know, I don't think a lot of that thought goes into it. And the third thing is sort of the don't place enough importance on what I hear a lot is, is that they don't like to do it. And so, you know, if you consistently come to the table with, you know, I hate to exercise, I hate to exercise, I hate to exercise, I mean, trying to get yourself to exercise is a thousand times worse than if you weren't telling yourself every five minutes, I hate to exercise. I mean, it's bad enough as it is without programming yourself that it's even worse. So, you know, it's like, I mean, I believe in the programming stuff. So like the racing, people ask me all the time, you mean, you know, you drive in the cold and the, I like driving in the cold. I mean, I don't like driving in the mud too much because I can't see, but I like driving in the cold in part because a lot of other guys don't like driving in the cold. So they're pissing and moaning and thinking negatively about it. I do better in the cold. I'm happy. I can bundle up. So I'm not programming myself that it's awful. You know, it's 20 below. Great. Good. Let's go race, you know. And so, I don't know for sure if I like doing it or not. I've convinced myself I like doing it. You know, I don't know really if that's true or not. And it would take years of therapy probably to determine whether or not that's reality. But, you know, I think you got to either convince yourself that, you know, the really important things, you know, are stuff you want to do, or you got to find somebody to delegate it to who's really, really, really good at it. And this is a piece that's really hard to delegate because it's the extension of your personality. Now, you can cheat at some just like I've cheated it now. So Bill does a page. Yeah, I did a page. So that's reduced my overall load. But you can't cheat it entirely. And I don't know how to shorten that list a lot because the game is to try and hit every one of those bases with different weighting, maybe month to month, but to hit every one of those bases. And the weekly facts are tougher. But if you look at like a month in aggregate, you'll find I'm pretty good at running through the 31 list and running through the alpha list. And anyone given facts, probably not. You write the gold and the gold plus. Yeah. I write what I write of this less the obvious and disclosed. I write the gold. I write the gold plus. And I write the weekly facts. And I write the gold and the gold plus. Yeah, there's two different inserts. Two different inserts, two different audios. Thematic leaders. Yeah, I try and do more advanced stuff, either strategy or philosophy in the gold plus than in the gold. Is this a strategic decision? You've got this box. Remember that your gold and gold plus, loose litter, blah, blah, blah, blah are set separately. Did you decide that you wanted like frequency, media, communication? Oh yeah. That really was Bill's decision. Well, there's two questions within your question. One is about the actual little box there on the page. No, well, maybe it's obvious. Why do you think it's there? Because the non-gold and gold plus guys think it's worth, you know. Big up. There you go. Yeah. Now, initially it's there when you make the change to actually remind them. Yeah. Right. But it stays there because it's reminding them that there's shit they ain't getting. But the separation of the stuff is, so now sending it to you two different times a month instead of one time a month, which costs more by the way, of course, is to try and counter the overwhelm factor. See, like the number one reason we lose people. There's nothing because they don't like us to like you. No, they can't keep up with it. They can't keep up. The stuff's piled up on the corner of the desk. They haven't read last months. They start to feel guilty and ashamed and, you know, unpleasant and then they want to get away from that. And see, it's not just my stuff on the corner. It's my stuff plus your stuff plus his stuff plus that guy's stuff plus that guy's stuff. All piled up on the same guy's corner. Pretty soon there's a stack so high if it topples over, it kills the dog. So, you know, that's not good. So, they get this big envelope of shit. They say, oh, I'll never get through this tonight. And so they set aside the whole big envelope. So our decision is trying to counter that reaction and give it to them in more digestible pieces. And it's funny. I thought about it the other day. Years ago in Success Track, when we used to dole a product out to them, we sent them two audio cassettes a month as their program, but we sent them one on the first and one on the 15th. So, first of all, retention is much higher, but we've done 18 things to make that happen. But I'll tell you something interesting about that is that one of the other big evanges of the newsletter is, you own your own media. And now we have, we own twice a month media. So we get more opportunity to do more things now because we have two important pieces of media versus one important piece of media. 188, dealing with the patently obvious topic of word choice. So we won't spend an enormous amount of time on it, but on page 189, there is a little useful checklist for you for fans of checklists. And that is to help you change one word into different words. So the example I used here is what do we do about thin? And although it's a little antiquated, I suppose, but one of the automatic things that most people did was you would hear thin as a rail, thin as a rail, thin as a rail. So that is a dime. So you look at each word and you have a choice of the cliche, you have a choice of the obvious extension of it, the literal description of it, some odd description of it, some extremely poetic description of it, some description of it that people are going to have to stop and think about a little, some cultural reference, some political reference, make it funny. Or if you choose, take some enormous risk with it. We're not going to do this exercise, but you ought to do it on your own on page 190, and you ought to actually do it. The useful thing to do it with is with words that you use a lot in your own writing. And then you build yourself sort of your own.
